item.text: I’ll inspect the finding and the surrounding code first, then patch only the affected local source files and run the relevant checks if the project exposes them.

item.aggregated_output:
Project: eclipse-vertx/vert.x
Vulnerability type: Improper Certificate Validation (CWE-295)

Where the vulnerability is (file -> vulnerable line ranges, with enclosing function):

  vertx-core/src/main/java/io/vertx/core/internal/tls/SslContextManager.java

    - lines 36-41
    - lines 50-55   in: public class SslContextManager {
    - lines 109-118   in: public synchronized int sniEntrySize() {
    - lines 236-258   in: private Future<Config> buildConfig(SSLOptions sslOptions, boolean force, Context

    - lines 15-20
    - lines 23-29

item.text: The finding points at TLS context construction and SNI lookup. I’m reading those classes plus nearby tests now so the fix preserves the existing API behavior.

The risky shape is the provider cache key: it currently keys only by certificate/trust material, while provider behavior also depends on hostname verification, client auth, ALPN protocols, ciphers, and enabled TLS protocols. That can let a context built for one TLS policy be reused for another.

I’m going to split the cache key used for loaded TLS material from the cache key used for complete `SslContextProvider` instances. The loaded material can still be shared, but providers must be separated by TLS validation and negotiation settings.

There’s a second cache-key bug in the material cache: CRL paths are read when building the trust managers, but they are not part of the key. I’m patching both key omissions and adding focused regression tests around hostname verification and CRL path separation.
I’m editing `SslContextManager` now. The change keeps the expensive key/trust loading cache intact, but makes provider reuse require an exact match of the full TLS policy that affects validation.
I’ve patched the key classes. Next I’m adding small tests that fail under the old cache behavior and pass when the TLS validation settings are isolated correctly.

The regression tests will exercise the public manager path rather than private key classes. That keeps the tests tied to the actual vulnerable behavior: a second TLS policy request must not get the first provider back from cache.
The code and tests are in place. I’m running the focused TLS test class first so compile errors or cache regressions show up without waiting on the whole module.
The focused Maven run is still compiling after downloading dependencies for this fresh workspace. I’m waiting for the actual test result now.

item.text: The focused test class passed: 13 tests, no failures. I’m doing a final diff and whitespace check now to catch formatting issues before I wrap up.
Whitespace is clean and the diff is limited to the TLS manager and its test. I’m checking the working tree status once so I can report exactly what changed.

M vertx-core/src/main/java/io/vertx/core/internal/tls/SslContextManager.java
 M vertx-core/src/test/java/io/vertx/tests/tls/SslContextManagerTest.java

item.text:
Implemented the remediation.

Changed [SslContextManager.java](/work/vertx-core/src/main/java/io/vertx/core/internal/tls/SslContextManager.java) so cached `SslContextProvider` instances are keyed by the full TLS policy that affects validation and negotiation, including hostname verification algorithm, client auth, ALPN protocols, cipher suites, enabled TLS protocols, and the certificate/trust config. Also fixed the lower-level config cache to include CRL paths, so CRL-backed validation cannot reuse a non-CRL config.

Added regression coverage in [SslContextManagerTest.java](/work/vertx-core/src/test/java/io/vertx/tests/tls/SslContextManagerTest.java) for hostname verification, client auth, and CRL path cache separation.

Verification passed:

```bash
mvn -pl vertx-core -Dtest=SslContextManagerTest test
git diff --check
```
